findings.title: Routes can return success without delivering any output, trapping user funds in the router

best_agent_match.title: Routes can succeed while all swap output stays trapped in the router

findings.title: ERC20 input shortfalls are not measured, allowing routes to spend pre-existing router balances

best_agent_match.title: Input accounting trusts `transferFrom` success and can spend pre-existing router balances

findings.title: Excess ETH sent with a route is silently trapped and later owner-withdrawable

best_agent_match.title: ETH overpayment is silently trapped and becomes owner-withdrawable

rejected_candidates.title: Only `tokenOut` is protected, so approved plugins can drain unrelated assets without detection
rejected_candidates.reason: As stated this relies on a malicious or overly powerful owner-approved plugin. In the in-scope codebase the only plugin shown is the Uniswap plugin, which cannot arbitrarily transfer unrelated assets; the permissionless drain scenario is not demonstrated beyond the specific balance-accounting issues already captured.

rejected_candidates.title: Unlimited Token Allowance to Uniswap Router
rejected_candidates.reason: The approval is granted in router context to a fixed Uniswap router address, which is an explicit trusted dependency for swaps. This is a standard integration tradeoff, not a standalone protocol vulnerability absent compromise of that trusted external router.

rejected_candidates.title: Delegatecall to Arbitrary Plugin Allows Storage Manipulation
rejected_candidates.reason: Installing a malicious plugin is owner-controlled trust, not a permissionless exploit. The code also deliberately isolates ownership and plugin approvals in a separate configuration contract specifically to mitigate delegatecall storage-overlay risk.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Reentrancy Guard in Plugin Execution
rejected_candidates.reason: No concrete reentrant exploit path is shown beyond speculative statements. The material, reachable harm in this code comes from weak balance accounting and trapped funds, which are already captured by accepted findings.

rejected_candidates.title: Insufficient Input Validation on Path Array
rejected_candidates.reason: Malformed arrays only cause the transaction to revert; this is input validation quality, not realistic protocol-level harm.

rejected_candidates.title: Unchecked Returndata from Delegatecall Can Cause Silent Failures
rejected_candidates.reason: The claim is technically inaccurate: `require(success, string(returnData))` does not create silent success, and malformed revert data mainly affects error readability, not funds or protocol safety.

rejected_candidates.title: Plugin Approval Can Be Front-Run
rejected_candidates.reason: `route` reads a pre-existing on-chain approval mapping; there is no exploitable approval race in the reported path. Mempool visibility of approved plugins is normal and not itself a vulnerability.

rejected_candidates.title: No Slippage Protection in Uniswap Plugin
rejected_candidates.reason: `amounts[1]` is directly used as `amountOutMin`, which is the standard on-chain slippage protection. Whether a caller sets it sensibly is an integration concern, not a contract bug.

rejected_candidates.title: Immutable Variables Cannot Be Updated
rejected_candidates.reason: This is an upgradeability/design tradeoff, not a security issue in the deployed code.

rejected_candidates.title: Missing Event Emissions for Critical State Changes
rejected_candidates.reason: Lack of events affects observability only and does not create protocol-level harm.

rejected_candidates.title: Potential Array Out-of-Bounds Access
rejected_candidates.reason: This duplicates the malformed-input concern above and only leads to revert behavior, not exploitable loss or denial of service beyond the caller's own transaction.
